title: The Moon's Ledger
description: Wren Allowyn ran from the Circus of the Moon before her first contract was done. Now the troupe keeps a way-house in the Reach, and in its ledger is a page with her old name on it. Break in, take the page, and get out — past the wires, the sweep, and the one they sent to wait for her. A heist of nerve, not steel.
introduction:
Every Circus performer is trained for the other work — the quiet feet, the wires, the trick of making a room betray the people in it. Wren learned all of it and walked out the dressing-room door on the night of her first contract. The Circus does not chase a runner. It waits, in the way only the Circus knows how, until the runner is somewhere where being found will hurt the most.

It has waited two years. Now there is a way-house out past the toll road with a ledger inside, and on one of its pages is the name Wren answered to before "Wren" — and the contract she abandoned, in ink, with a price. She means to take that page back before the troupe uses it to collect.

There will be no crowd tonight, and no rope between two balconies. Only a dark approach, a rigged corridor, a house that wakes, and a room with someone already in it. Everything the Circus made her is about to be turned against the Circus.
aiContext: THE MOON'S LEDGER is a four-chapter solo infiltration adventure for Wren Allowyn (Halfling Rogue, levels 1–4). It is the showcase for three mechanics, one per chapter, combined in the finale: (1) STEALTH + AMBUSH — Wren's Expertise-grade Stealth plus the Hide/Sneak-Attack loop; (2) TRAP DETECTION + DISARM — concealed tile traps spotted via Perception/Search and removed via the Disarm action (Dexterity / Sleight of Hand, Advantage with Thieves' Tools); (3) AREA-DENIAL GEAR — caltrops and ball bearings deployed as hazard zones that render like spell effects. Chapter 1 (The Cold Approach) teaches stealth against a lone sentry. Chapter 2 (Tripwires & Tinkers) teaches detect/disarm in a rigged corridor. Chapter 3 (Her Own Tricks) teaches offensive gear use against a two-person sweep. Chapter 4 (The Ledger Room) combines all three: a hidden enforcer, a trapped ledger case, and room to seed hazards. Tone: tense, quiet, competent — a runner finishing a job. The thematic thread is that Wren is using the Circus's own training to escape the Circus; the enforcer in the final room is what the troupe does to those who try to leave. Keep lethality low; reward cleverness (a clean ambush, a disarmed trap, a well-placed caltrop) over brute force. Wren begins equipped for the work — Thieves' Tools, caltrops, ball bearings, crowbar, rope.
